title: Rule of, or by, law

audience_match: Inquirer Opinion readers concerned with democratic backsliding
Lawyers and constitutional academics following Charter-change debates

primary_topics: Distinction between RULE OF LAW (laws as social contracts protecting human rights and freedom) and RULE BY LAW (laws as instruments imposed by rulers to enforce their will)
Cultural-historical analysis of Filipino lawlessness — feudal society + impunity for the powerful + 'batas as decree' linguistic inheritance
World Justice Project's Rule of Law Index — methodology (9 factors, 44 subfactors), four universal principles, general population poll
Philippines's alarming drop in WJP rankings — 51st of 102 (2015) ? 70th of 113 (2016) ? 88th of 113 (recent) — 'the worst in the world'
Comparative-rankings analysis — China 75th, Singapore 13th; lowest-ranked Venezuela and Cambodia also have one-party rule (so authoritarianism alone doesn't explain)
The Davao police incident (January 21) where three minors were arrested, mauled, and made to kneel apologizing to Duterte's photograph — symbol of rule-of-law dismantling
Concern about Charter change concentrating power away from legislature, judiciary, and media
sub_topics: Tagalog 'batas' etymology — 'to decree'; something imposed by rulers/the powerful
English 'law' from old Norse — 'that which is set, or laid down'
Feudal-era law as codified system defining what rulers want and prescribing punishments for going against ruler's will
Modern (last three centuries) shift to rule of law alongside human rights and freedom
WJP since 2010 — annual Rule of Law Index reports
Methodology — 9 factors ? 44 subfactors ? identified by academics, practitioners, community leaders
Four WJP universal principles: accountability, just laws, open government, accessible and impartial dispute resolution
Open government — 'processes by which the laws are enacted, administered, and enforced are accessible, fair and efficient'
General population poll — 1,000 respondents in Manila, Cebu, Davao for Philippines
Qualified respondents questionnaires — experts in civil/commercial law, criminal justice, labor law, public health
Examples — home burglary experience question + reporting behavior
Venezuela and Cambodia — lowest-ranked, both one-party rule
Marcos martial law era as Philippine example of rule-by-law as economic ruin
January 21 Davao police incident: three minors arrested, mauled, made to kneel before Duterte's photograph
WJP yearbook: 'effective rule of law reduces corruption, combats poverty and disease, and protects people from injustices large and small'
Reference to mass transit disaster and vaccine scandal as squandered-public-funds examples
Charter-change concerns: power being taken away from legislature, judiciary, civil society/media; risk of 'local warlords' enforcing rule-by-law

signature_phrases.phrase: we live in a terribly feudal society that encourages impunity
People in power and people who ride on power can flaunt the law and get away with it
If no one's watching, then let's try to get away with breaking it
Our drop of 18 places was the worst in the world
even in authoritarian countries, you might find forms of checks and balances
The totalitarian temptation is always there
effective rule of law reduces corruption, combats poverty and disease, and protects people from injustices large and small
to the edge of the precipice
an almost playful cat-and-mouse routine to an acceptance of unlawful arrest, detention and execution
Who wins, who loses, in the long run?

people: President Rodrigo Duterte (referenced via Davao police incident, Charter-change agenda)
Ferdinand Marcos Sr. (referenced via martial law era)
institutions: Philippine legislature, judiciary, civil society, media (referenced generally)
events: January 21 (2018) Davao police incident with three minors
WJP Rule of Law Index reports — annual since 2010
Recent WJP rankings: PH 51st of 102 (2015), 70th of 113 (2016), 88th of 113 (2018)

stances.claim: Filipino lawlessness reflects more than weak discipline — it reflects a feudal society that encourages impunity (the powerful flaunt law and get away; the rest treat law as a game) and a linguistic inheritance where 'batas' literally means 'to decree' (something imposed by rulers, by the powerful). Modern rule of law as social contract developed only in the last three centuries alongside human rights and freedom.

The Philippines's dramatic drop in the WJP Rule of Law Index — 51st (2015) ? 70th (2016) ? 88th of 113 — represents 'the worst drop in the world.' Comparative analysis disproves the simple 'authoritarianism causes good rule-of-law performance' thesis: while Singapore (13th) and China (75th) outrank PH despite authoritarian characteristics, Venezuela and Cambodia — both one-party — are lowest-ranked. Even authoritarian regimes vary; what matters is whether checks and balances function.
stances.rhetorical_move: comparative-rankings analysis disproving simple authoritarianism thesis

stances.claim: If law is used to amass more power for a despot and his clique — rule BY law — the country will move quickly toward economic ruin, as the Marcos martial law era demonstrated. Filipinos who view law as an obstacle to development are susceptible to the totalitarian temptation, which becomes especially alluring in times of crisis. But the WJP findings remind us that effective rule of law actually 'reduces corruption, combats poverty and disease, and protects people from injustices large and small.'
stances.confidence: asserted with historical and empirical backing

stances.claim: The January 21, 2018 incident in Davao — three minors arrested, mauled, and made to kneel apologizing to President Duterte's photograph — shows the dismantling of rule of law moving from 'an almost playful cat-and-mouse routine to an acceptance of unlawful arrest, detention and execution.' Nothing should be considered too trivial in such dismantling.

Charter change is concerning because it threatens to take power away from the legislature, judiciary, and civil-society media. The critical questions are: Who wins, who loses, in the long run? Will rule of law be strengthened, or will it shift further toward rule BY law, particularly by local warlords?
stances.rhetorical_move: rhetorical-question stack closer about Charter change

notable_anecdotes.summary: Etymology lesson: Tagalog 'batas' actually means 'to decree' — something imposed by rulers; English 'law' from old Norse means 'that which is set, or laid down.' Both languages embed a top-down, ruler-imposed origin.
Religion analogy for Filipino law-as-game culture: 'good behavior tied to God, or a CCTV, watching. If the coast is clear, then make a run for it.'
Counter-intuitive comparative finding: 'Would you believe China ranked 75th in the latest report? Singapore, which we associate with authoritarianism, ranked 13th.'
WJP general-population-poll method using everyday experiences (e.g., home-burglary experience + reporting behavior) to gauge people's perception of legal system.
January 21 Davao police incident: three minors arrested, mauled, made to kneel apologizing to a photograph of President Duterte.
Mass transit disaster and vaccine scandal as concrete instances of rule-of-law erosion squandering public funds and lives.

decision_framework_signals: uses the rule-OF-law vs rule-BY-law distinction as the master diagnostic frame
applies cultural-linguistic etymology as cultural-pathology diagnosis tool
treats WJP Rule of Law Index data as authoritative empirical anchor
uses comparative-rankings to disprove simple authoritarianism-causes-good-governance thesis
applies Marcos-era historical lesson as warning against totalitarian temptation
treats specific incidents (Davao minors, mass transit, vaccine scandal) as symptoms of structural rule-of-law erosion
opens Charter-change with stakes-and-winners-losers analytical frame
target_audience: Inquirer Opinion readers concerned with democratic backsliding
Lawyers and constitutional academics following Charter-change debates
register_markers: alternative-cultural-theory framing ('alternative mix of cultural and social theories')
casual interjection register ('Would you believe China ranked 75th?')
one_paragraph_summary: Published February 2, 2018, this column dissects the difference between RULE OF LAW (laws as social contracts protecting human rights and freedom) and RULE BY LAW (laws as instruments imposed by rulers to enforce their will). CJP traces the cultural origins of Filipino lawlessness — a feudal society that encourages impunity (the powerful flaunt the law and get away; the rest treat law as a game) — and diagnoses the linguistic inheritance: Tagalog 'batas' actually means 'to decree,' something imposed by rulers; English 'law' from old Norse means 'that which is set, or laid down.' Modern rule of law as social contract developed only in the last three centuries alongside human rights and freedom. The World Justice Project's Rule of Law Index — annual since 2010, with 9 factors broken into 44 subfactors, four universal principles (accountability, just laws, open government, accessible and impartial dispute resolution), and a general-population poll of 1,000 respondents in Manila, Cebu, and Davao — shows the Philippines dropping dramatically: 51st of 102 (2015) ? 70th of 113 (2016) ? 88th of 113 in the latest report. 'Our drop of 18 places was the worst in the world.' Comparative analysis disproves the simple authoritarianism-causes-good-governance thesis: China ranks 75th and Singapore 13th, but the lowest-ranked countries (Venezuela and Cambodia) also have one-party rule. What matters is whether checks and balances function. The Marcos martial law era is the Philippine cautionary tale of rule BY law leading to economic ruin. The totalitarian temptation grows in crisis. The January 21, 2018 Davao police incident — three minors arrested, mauled, made to kneel apologizing to President Duterte's photograph — shows the dismantling of rule of law moving 'from an almost playful cat-and-mouse routine to an acceptance of unlawful arrest, detention and execution.' Mass transit disasters and the vaccine scandal are concrete cases of squandered public funds and endangered lives. The column closes with stakes-and-winners-losers questions about ongoing Charter-change debates: will rule of law be strengthened, or will rule BY law — particularly by local warlords — gain ground?
